original_abstract: As the world’s most popular spectator sport, soccer is a global stage where diverse cultures converge around a single goal. Beyond the pitch, the game has evolved since the late nineteenth century into a profound social phenomenon. Scholars globally have long used soccer as a lens through which to examine the complexities of modern life, including its intersection with politics, economics, religion, media, and aesthetics. *Soccer & Society*, the first international journal dedicated exclusively to the sport, explores the game within the context of an increasingly globalized world. By integrating anthropological, historical, sociological, and economic perspectives, the journal provides an interdisciplinary forum for analyzing the deepening relationship between soccer and the vital structures of society. Committed to a truly international scope, *Soccer & Society* seeks to foster original empirical research from scholars across the globe, bridging the gap between sport and the human experience.
humanized_abstract: Examining the beautiful game in a global civilisation, where cultures collide on the soccer pitch. Soccer is played in almost every country in the world, and makes a whole host of people very, very happy and very, very sad. It started life as a pretty mundane sporting activity, in the late nineteenth century, but rapidly became a major sociocultural activity in societies across the world. As such, since the late nineteenth century, soccer and all its permutations, has been studied by scholars all over the world, as an indicator of a range of sociocultural phenomena, from the political, to the economic, to the religious, to media and aesthetic levels, etc. Soccer & Society, the first ever international journal of sociology of soccer (or football as it is known in the rest of the world) is a fully refereed, global, interdisciplinary, academic journal which explores the sociocultural development of the game on a global level through a sociologically framed analysis of the dynamics of the relationship between sport and society, and vice versa. This journal looks to bridge the space between sport and society, exploring the dynamics and relationships between the games and the world they are played in. It is truly international, and welcomes, in English, alternative and innovative academic empirical work from any scholar in the world.
